topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence of the coffee shop amplified every awkward shuffle of my feet. Liam was laughing, a bright, easy sound that used to be my favorite soundtrack, and I watched him, a knot tightening in my chest. He was talking animatedly about a shared memory, a trip to the coast from years ago, and then he paused, a flicker of something distant crossing his eyes. "Wait," he said, turning slightly, his gaze sweeping over the room, and then… nothing. He shook his head, a small, dismissive gesture, and moved on. The air left my lungs in a sharp, shallow gasp. I clutched my lukewarm mug so tightly my knuckles turned white. It wasn't just a fleeting moment; it felt like a physical blow, the foundation of a shared history suddenly dissolving into mist. I wanted to apologize, to reach out, but the words were lodged somewhere behind my teeth, thick and unyielding. I just stood there, a statue of stunned disappointment, feeling the heat rise in my cheeks, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I replayed the evening in my head a hundred times, trying to find the misstep, the careless word that had pushed him away. It was the way I’d been so certain our bond was unbreakable, so vital to my world. Now, sitting alone in my cramped apartment, the echo of his indifference was deafening. I picked up the old photo album, the one filled with grainy snapshots of our teenage adventures, and traced the outline of his grinning face. It felt like handling something fragile, something I had carelessly damaged. A slow, heavy ache settled in my stomach, a dull, persistent throb that made breathing shallow. I wanted to call him, to demand an explanation, but the thought of hearing a cold, factual denial made my throat constrict. Instead, I just sat, staring at the glossy paper, letting the quiet weight of my failure settle over me like a shroud.

[story 3]

When I finally found him by the campus quad, the sunlight felt abrasive, exposing the raw vulnerability churning inside me. He looked up, his expression open and friendly, exactly as it always had. "Hey, Sarah," he greeted me, a warm smile spreading across his face. I managed a weak nod, my voice sounding rusty and foreign. I tried to launch into a casual conversation, something about the upcoming project, but the words caught. I searched for the familiar shorthand, the inside jokes that used to bridge the gap between us, but they were gone, replaced by an unnerving blankness in his eyes. He answered politely, but the connection felt severed, like trying to hold water in cupped hands. Every attempt I made to bridge the distance only seemed to widen the chasm. I felt a profound sense of emptiness bloom in my chest, a heavy, suffocating pressure that made me want to simply disappear into the noisy crowd.
